stimulus: Robin Hood legends—tales centered on the figure of the outlaw archer Robin Hood—come down to us from a number of sources, including the ballad Robin Hood and the Monk, preserved in a manuscript from around 1450, and the printed compilation A Gest of Robyn Hode, dating to roughly 1495. One of the most influential sources, however, was the chronicler John Major’s History of Greater Britain, written in 1521; portions of that work were later reworked by the playwright Anthony Munday into the play The Downfall of Robert, Earl of Huntington in 1598. But Munday didn’t simply adapt Major’s material—he added to it as well, introducing for the first time the idea that Robin Hood was a dispossessed nobleman of the Huntington line, an idea that became central to later versions of the legend. From this, it follows that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: John Major was unaware of stories of Robin Hood’s noble origins when writing History of Greater Britain, although historians know that texts containing such stories were already in circulation at that time.
The Downfall of Robert, Earl of Huntington is more historically accurate than History of Greater Britain, because A Gest of Robyn Hode had not yet been printed when Major composed his chronicle.
the Robin Hood legends drawn on by the composer of Robin Hood and the Monk would not have depicted Robin Hood as a dispossessed nobleman of the Huntington line.

John Major’s accounts of Robin Hood in History of Greater Britain are more similar in overall content to the accounts in The Downfall of Robert, Earl of Huntington than to those in Robin Hood and the Monk.

rationale:
Choice C is the best answer because it most logically follows from the text. The text says Munday, writing in 1598, was the first to introduce the idea that Robin Hood was a dispossessed nobleman of the Huntington line. Robin Hood and the Monk, however, was preserved in a manuscript from about 1450—well before Munday's innovation. So the legends available to the composer of Robin Hood and the Monk could not yet have included the dispossessed-nobleman idea.

Choice A is incorrect because the text says Munday introduced the noble-origin idea for the first time, which rules out the existence of earlier texts containing such stories. Choice B is incorrect because the text says nothing about historical accuracy, and the relative dates of A Gest of Robyn Hode and Major's chronicle don't bear on accuracy. Choice D is incorrect because the text gives no basis for comparing the overall content of Major's chronicle with that of either later work.
